claim_status: No-claim technology and dependency source observations only; no language, framework, dependency, runtime, source strategy, license, provenance, security, performance, or release selection or approval follows.

sources.title: Unsafety - The Rust Reference
sources.observation: Rust identifies operations that can violate memory-safety guarantees and keeps raw pointers, unsafe calls, unsafe traits, extern declarations, and related operations outside the safe subset.
sources.decision_consequence: A candidate foundation must inventory unsafe, FFI, platform-handle, allocator, JIT, codec, and generated-code boundaries; a Rust language choice alone is not safety evidence.

sources.title: Cargo Reference
sources.observation: Cargo separates manifests, workspaces, dependency specifications, source replacement, dependency resolution, features, profiles, build scripts, registries, and package identity.
sources.decision_consequence: A dependency decision must record the complete configuration and resolution, not only a lockfile or package list, and must bind optional features and native/build-time inputs to the evidence record.

sources.title: Source Replacement
sources.observation: Cargo source replacement supports vendoring and mirroring and relies on the replacement source containing exactly the same source as the source it replaces.
sources.decision_consequence: A source policy must preserve source identity, checksums or equivalent content identity, mirror/vendor configuration, retrieval provenance, and rejection behavior for mismatches; replacement is not an implicit patch or private-registry policy.

sources.title: Build Scripts
sources.observation: Cargo build scripts can compile non-Rust code, discover system libraries, generate code, and perform platform-specific configuration before a package builds.
sources.decision_consequence: Build scripts and their compiler, linker, filesystem, environment, process, network, and native-library side effects are part of the dependency and authority boundary and require explicit inventories and replay evidence.

sources.title: SPDX overview and license list
sources.observation: SPDX provides standardized identifiers and exchange formats for software-license and supply-chain information.
sources.decision_consequence: An SPDX expression improves machine-readable inventory but must remain paired with exact observed license text, notices, source-offer obligations, patent or codec flags, list version, and legal-community review.

sources.title: SPDX 3.0.1 specification
sources.observation: The SPDX specification defines structured package, file, relationship, licensing, and provenance concepts for exchanging software supply-chain information.
sources.decision_consequence: The selected dependency record must bind SBOM entities and relationships to exact source revisions, generated outputs, native artifacts, notices, and build identity; an SBOM does not itself approve distribution.

sources.title: SLSA security levels
sources.observation: SLSA describes increasing provenance and build-platform expectations and distinguishes provenance properties from an absolute guarantee of artifact safety.
sources.decision_consequence: A candidate foundation must state the provenance level and its limits, retain builder and input identity, and avoid treating a provenance record as a substitute for source review, reproducibility, security, or release approval.

unsupported: No language, framework, dependency, runtime, graphics stack, codec, storage engine, or source baseline is selected.
No license, patent, codec, SBOM, advisory, maintenance, reproducibility, or release decision is approved.
No clean-host dependency replay, independent build, benchmark, security, compatibility, accessibility, or production evidence exists in this manifest.
No broad implementation, Chrome-class, extreme-performance, or release claim follows from these source observations.
